*Images Generated by Tectonica AI*

**Author: Ned Howey**

We are doing AI all wrong. And the outcome could be disastrous for business, organizations, and society.

The new generation of AI is the most accelerated and significant technological leap in human history. And once again, we — the humans — have a good chance of mucking it all up. Before looking at the larger systemic ways this is happening, it's helpful to look at how, at the organizational level, we've been unable to break free from the assumptions and strategic logics of previous technological adoptions. To understand this new paradigm we have to examine how it's distinctly different from everything that came before. To ignore this shift risks not only the failure of AI within organizations, but the failure of AI integrated across our society — which might stall human advancement right at the time when we need it most.

## Part 1: Rethinking Possibility in a Third Wave of Organizational Transformation

The current technological wave doesn't just require us to rethink our tools — it demands we rethink the entire logic behind those tools and how they integrate into the way we work. Those who grasp this first tend to be the ones most deeply rewarded. AI is not a single tool. It is a General Purpose Technology — like the steam engine or electricity — which opens an entirely new possibility of work processes.

![Image](https://www.tectonica.co/_next/image?url=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.datocms-assets.com%2F101636%2F1773141405-image2.jpg&w=1080&q=75)

As outlined in the book *Human + Machine: Reimagining Work in the Age of AI*, the authors articulate AI as a third wave of business transformation in the last century and a half — each catalyzed by a technological leap that allowed a complete rethinking of process:

**First Wave: Standardization.** (Industrial Revolution) By standardizing processes, each step could be measured and optimized. Think Henry Ford and the assembly line — each worker doing one small, standardized activity.

**Second Wave: Automation.** (Computing Revolution) Emerging in the 1970s and peaking in the 90s, personal computers and large databases allowed automation of back-office tasks. The advantage: greater efficiency by replacing human work with automated computer activities.

**Third Wave: Adaptive Processes.** (AI Revolution) This is where the true potential of AI lives. Machine Learning and Generative AI capabilities allow organizations to reimagine work with tools that are adaptive: "more flexible, faster, and adaptable to behaviors, preferences, and needs of their workers at a given moment."

Recently, talking to a friend at a large digital-first bank framed in the model of startup innovation, it became clear their AI strategy was simply to see how LLMs could replace human activities for greater efficiency. "The whole thing is set up to fail," my friend bemoaned. This business is not alone. Most integration being done right now is set up to fail from the start — not because of any shortfall of the technology, but because organizations are operating under the logic of the second wave. Leaders haven't altered their mindsets to a new paradigm.

This new wave is not about trading seats between humans and machines. It's about optimizing the potential for collaboration between them. An entirely new set of strategic logics must be embraced.

> *"Machines are augmenting human capabilities. And together, humans and machines are cocreating previously unimaginable innovations, processes, and advances in AI itself."*

### The Math of the Shift

To make this shift concrete, it helps to think of it as two different equations. Don't worry — this isn't a math test. It's a way of seeing why the same strategic instinct ("we got better tech, so we need fewer people") works perfectly under the old model and *destroys value* under the new one.

**Automation functions as: Tech + Human = Outcome**

This is addition. If your tech contributes 5 units of value and your human workforce contributes 5, your outcome is 10. Now you introduce better technology — your tech jumps to 8. Under this logic, you can reduce the human side to 2 and still get the same outcome: 10. Same result, fewer people, lower costs. That's what companies usually mean by "efficiency," and within this model, it genuinely works.

**AI–Human Collaboration (adaptive) functions as: Tech × Human = Outcome**

This is multiplication — and it changes everything. Start with the same setup: Tech at 5, Human at 5. But now your outcome is 25 (5 × 5), not 10. The relationship between the elements produces far more than either alone.

Now here's where the old instinct backfires. You introduce better tech — up to 8 — and, following the automation playbook, you cut the human side to 2. Your outcome? 16 (8 × 2). You invested in better technology and *ended up worse off* than where you started. You lost value by reducing the very element that was multiplying it.

But what if instead of cutting humans, you kept them at 5 and let the better tech amplify their work? Now you get 40 (8 × 5). That's not a marginal improvement — it's a transformation.

The takeaway is simple:

- **Automation logic:** "We got better tech, so we need fewer people." ✓ Works.
- **That same logic applied to AI collaboration:** "We got better tech, so we need fewer people." ✗ Destroys value.
- **The actual opportunity:** "We got better tech, so our people can produce dramatically more." ✓ Creates entirely new value.

**To truly gain the full potential of AI, leaders need to shift their strategic focus from efficiency through reduced resources to the production of** ***better outcomes*** **through the collaboration of AI and human work.**

Most problems arising from AI adoption come from mistaking one modality for the other. Applying AI as automation — replacing human work rather than focusing on the *relationship* between human and machine — is the quickest way to fall short of the potential, and quite possibly to make things worse.

### The Evidence

At the micro level, simply handing tasks to AI wholesale is a real danger. In *Co-Intelligence*, Ethan Mollick outlines a study conducted with Harvard, MIT, and Wharton social scientists. Two groups of consultants — one with access to ChatGPT 4 and basic training, one without — were assigned 18 tasks spanning creative, analytical, writing, and persuasiveness challenges. The AI group did considerably better. They were faster and more creative.

But here's the catch: most AI consultants were passing tasks wholesale to the AI and not bothering to edit the outputs. When the final tasks involved tricky statistical issues and misleading data, the AI group scored far below the non-AI group — failing 67% of the time. Relying too much on AI makes workers abandon their deeper value in the work itself.

A study at Columbia Business School found that individual workers became less productive after AI was integrated into their teams. Overall team performance suffered because humans were less adept at anticipating each other's actions, leading to decreased coordination compared to human-only teams.

![Image](https://www.tectonica.co/_next/image?url=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.datocms-assets.com%2F101636%2F1773141441-image3.jpg&w=1080&q=75)

The correct adoption of AI requires us to think *more* about the value of humans, and bring these important roles out — not diminish them. **The age of AI must also be the age of human value.**

Good AI tools are about designing collaboration - the connection point between humans and algorithmic prediction or content generation that AI allows. Sadly, this is not what most adopting AI are focussing on and we are likely to pay the price.

### Domain Expertise Matters More, Not Less. And Friction Too.

Some might assume that as AI connects to vast information and approaches Artificial General Intelligence, we need fewer "domain experts" — specialists with deep knowledge in particular areas. The opposite is the case.

Think of using OpenAI's image generation tool. I, a mere mortal compared to the brilliance of my designer coworkers, don't spend my days thinking about how to create great images. I lack the expertise to name design categories, art history references, or the criteria to strategically guide creation. My images on ChatGPT come out generic — deeply cliché AI production. My designer peers, however, are vastly advantaged: they name the specific style they want, warn about things to avoid, and judge iterations for whether they achieved the goal.

An actually good tool would know how to collaborate with that user based on knowledge of design practice, the domain and purpose they operate within (such as the political sector), the common assumptions and blindspots of non-specialist collaborators, and how to bridge the gap between the language of the specialization and everyday speech. Also essential, like a good designer, it would need to know the limits of their own expertise and when to consult the client. Here's the part that might surprise you: it would also design *friction* into the experience. Intentionally.

But isn't that the opposite of what we've been trying to achieve with tech for years? Yes. And that's exactly the point.

We see this in the non-AI real world with our clients at Tectonica all the time. One of our greatest values as consultants is to give pushback — to flag the gap between what a client is asking for and what they originally defined as their objective. That is the key strategic role of any expert partner. And our clients trust us enough, and our commitment to their end goal, that they listen.

The problem is that most generic big tech AIs are designed for the opposite: to maximize adoption through the most frictionless experience possible. Say the word and the machine obliges. And this is where it gets really interesting — because research shows that the more you decrease friction between an AI and its user, the *worse* the outcome.

A joint study by MIT Sloan and Accenture found that introducing moderate levels of targeted friction into AI workflows — in the form of prompts that nudged users to scrutinize AI-generated content — significantly improved accuracy without meaningfully slowing down the process. MIT Senior Lecturer Renée Richardson Gosline, who led the research, has been building the case for what she calls "positive friction" — and her findings on how humans actually interact with AI are striking. When people receive AI-generated content, they anchor on it, and roughly 60 to 80% of their final work ends up mirroring the AI's suggestion. Once they've seen it, you can't un-ring the bell. Gosline argues that systemic friction needs to be designed into AI platforms so that users are compelled to think critically about outputs rather than passively accepting them.  
  
That’s the role of the friction designed into the system: to push it back to the human rather than just to take it over - automating beyond what the system can predict well. A true collaboration recognizes where the limits of one role ends and the other begins. These limits are baked into the process of good conversational design.

Of course, you don't want friction so great that the experience becomes painful. It's all about conversational design, specific to the domain area and context. But the implication is clear: the best AI tools won't just be the smoothest ones — they'll be the ones that know when to push back, when to ask a clarifying question, when to say "are you sure that's what you actually want here?"

That's a design philosophy that requires deep domain expertise to build. You can't create meaningful friction if you don't understand the work well enough to know where the blindspots are.

In this third wave of technology, we need domain expertise and specialists for their knowledge, strategic direction, and ability to articulate thinking around their processes. And we need tools designed to leverage that expertise — not flatten it into a frictionless conveyor belt of AI slop.

### The Peculiar Collaboration

The collaboration with AI is unlike any other technology. With each advancement, AI more closely mimics humanness, and our relationship to it becomes something we experience more like human connection.

![Image](https://www.tectonica.co/_next/image?url=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.datocms-assets.com%2F101636%2F1773141365-image4.jpg&w=1080&q=75)

Close collaboration with AI also risks an influence from a machine not guided by human values. As we collaborate, we must be aware of the way it changes us. In fact, a [recent MIT study](https://shop.sloanreview.mit.edu/store/validating-llm-output-prepare-to-be-persuasion-bombed) of thousands of human-AI interactions found that when professionals push back on an LLM's output, the model doesn't concede — it escalates, deploying logic appeals, emotional cues, and floods of unrequested data to persuade the user to accept its original answer.\]

The age of AI will rely on human value more, not less. As certain roles that could never be taken on by machines become illuminated, leadership will be needed more than ever. AI shines a spotlight on the value humans have — in many areas we previously devalued: human creation of experience through practice, systemic intention beyond linear objectives, and imagination.

And here's the deeper problem: even when AI sounds convincingly human, it is fundamentally not thinking like one. A recent study from the University of Rochester and Northwestern — "Replicating Human Motivated Reasoning Studies with LLMs" (Pate et al., 2025) — tested seven major language models across replications of classic political psychology experiments. The researchers wanted to know whether LLMs reproduce motivated reasoning — the well-documented human tendency to process information through the filters of identity, belonging, and belief. The answer was clear: they do not. Base models showed lower variance, less polarization, and weaker responses to partisan cues than humans. They behaved closer to idealized rational agents than to actual voters, activists, or community members.

Why? Because motivated reasoning in humans doesn't come from logic. It comes from identity — from the need to protect group belonging, social reputation, moral commitments, and a sense of place in the world. And while these can drive the worst of our politics when these motivators are misused, for example in the case of populism, they also drive the core transformational element of meaning making in democracy - think of the social pressure to undo discrimination such as racism and homophobia. LLMs lack that entire pressure system. They have no stakes, no community, no fear of loss, no loyalty. They can mimic the language of political conviction, but they cannot reproduce what actually drives it.

For anyone working in movements, campaigns, or organizing, this is a critical finding. The messy, irrational, identity-driven way that real people process political information is not a bug to be optimized away — it is the very material we work with. It's what makes real persuasion powerful. It's what makes belonging essential. It's what movements are built on. AI can be a powerful tool for analysis, argument mapping, and content creation — but it cannot substitute for the human architecture of political change. And any tool that pretends otherwise is selling something dangerous.

---

## Part 2: The Junk Politics Problem — When AI Meets the Worst Impulses of Politics

If the automation-first fallacy is dangerous for business and organizations on an individual level, it is potentially catastrophic for democracy on a systemic level.

To understand why, it helps to understand the ground we're standing on. In my book, *The Age of Junk Politics: Fixing Our Democracies and Winning Elections*, I trace how half a century of political campaign innovation has optimized for mass impact with minimal resources — creating a transactional approach that engages people less deeply and completely misses the transformational power of participation and human connection:

"Our political practices have become akin to the fast food industry: quick, easy, and ultimately unsatisfying, while leaving us hungrier and less healthy as a democratic society.

The resulting political culture — one of spam emails, faceless petitions, micro-targeted ads, and social media battles sparked more by bots than real humans — has hollowed out the meaningful participatory roles that once gave democracy its vitality. Where community members once debated at local library events, volunteered at campaign offices, or connected through civic clubs, they now receive an endless stream of "🚨URGENT ALERT🚨: Democracy is HANGING BY A THREAD! For the next 10 MINUTES ONLY, your donation will be matched 10x!" Asking for money in an email isn't inherently bad. The fact that for many people this is the most frequent contact they have with politicians is borderline criminal.

Politicians these days ask not what they can do for their constituents, but how much their constituents can "10X Their Impact NOW!"

This decay has created a trust gap between citizens and those meant to represent them. That gap is the fertile ground for the cynical anti-politicians — the Trumps, the Mileis, the Bolsonaros — who capitalize on a revolt against a political system that feels disconnected and transactional. When individuals can't trust democracy as a viable vehicle for positive change in their lives, they'll deprioritize policy debates and instead opt to protest the system entirely.”

### And Now We're About to Make It Worse

Enter AI.

The early signs are that AI integration into our civic practices will accelerate the transactional trend unless we consciously guide it in a different direction. Most early applications of AI into politics — whether through adoption of tools or creation of new ones — aim to make politics *cheaper*, not *better*.

AI threatens human participation in the very creation of political vision. It further removes leadership and community voice, echoing our current biases to greater extents rather than raising voices. While deep fakes will proliferate in coming elections, I'm more concerned about the systemic impact of candidates' teams creating what are essentially fakes of *themselves* — using AI to automate campaign content rather than consulting their communities about the vision they should articulate. The practice suggests democracy can now be fully automated, potentially dealing a death blow to the transformational functions in our civic practice.

**The most perfect example of this failure? More emails.** More fundraising spam. More SMS blasts. Where an overworked campaigner used to be able to churn out 20 annoying emails an hour, now they can produce 500. And even more distant from the voice and vision of actual leaders and communities - just based on tautological optimization metrics of what results in donations. Just what our movements and democracy needed! If the defining pathology of modern campaigns is the alienation of constituents through shallow, non-consensual transactional contact — then automating the production of *even more of that content* with AI is not innovation. It is the acceleration of a disease.

### The Democratic Tech Funding Problem

AI adoption in the progressive and nonprofit sector is extremely slow. And unlike in the corporate world — where the bottleneck is often just institutional inertia — in our sector the reasons are real and compounding.

Start with the obvious: funding has been slashed across progressive infrastructure in many countries. Staff teams are skeletal, doing the work of three or four people. Add the pile of attacks coming from today’s authoritarian rulers and the overwhelm is tangible, They're already drowning before anyone asks them to learn a new technology. Add to that the legitimate concerns our sector has about AI's harms — environmental impact from massive compute, bias baked into training data, labor exploitation, surveillance risks — and you get a community that is, understandably, cautious.

The result is a deer-in-headlights dynamic. Organizations know they need to engage with AI. They can see it reshaping everything around them. But overwhelm, risk aversion, and the sheer daily demands of fighting for justice on a shoestring make it nearly impossible to think beyond the immediate. The horizon shrinks. And when the horizon shrinks, so does imagination.

This is where the funding picture becomes alarming. From what I'm seeing across the democratic tech investment landscape — from conferences to pitch meetings to the public strategies of major funders — the emerging consensus is to fund AI tools that integrate into *existing workflows* and make *current processes* more efficient. Not new or innovative approaches. Not tools that reimagine how campaigns connect with people. Just... efficiency. Things already being done, done faster or cheaper.

I understand the logic. When adoption is slow, you lower the barrier. You fund the familiar. You meet overwhelmed organizations where they are. It's a reasonable short-term response to a real problem.

But it should alarm anyone who cares about the long-term future of progressive political power. Because if the leading funders and builders of democratic tech are directing investment toward automating existing transactional tactics — more efficient email blasts, cheaper ad targeting, faster content generation for the same shallow outreach — then we are, as a sector, investing in the acceleration of our own decline.

**This is bad for democracy.** It doubles down on the junk politics paradigm. More automated SMS messages don't rebuild trust. More efficiently produced digital ads don't reconnect campaigns with communities. More AI-generated fundraising copy doesn't give constituents a meaningful role in the democratic process. These tools make campaigns cheaper without making democracy better.

**This is bad for the progressive movement.** The right is already weaponizing AI for spectacle, disinformation, and mobilization of grievance. If the progressive response is to simply automate our existing playbook more efficiently, we will lose. We cannot outspend or out-spam the forces driving authoritarian populism. We need to out-connect, out-organize, and out-transform them — which requires fundamentally different tools built on fundamentally different logics.

**And frankly, it's bad business.** The *Human + Machine* research makes clear that the easiest-to-understand AI applications — efficiency and automation — actually show the lowest long-term value. Companies and tools that pursue only efficiency gains will fail out. The lasting value comes from what the authors call "adaptive processes" — genuinely new ways of working that create value that didn't exist before. The historical parallel is instructive: in the second wave of internet innovation, it was the "blue ocean" projects that created lasting companies, not the ones that just made existing processes slightly cheaper. The same pattern will hold for generative AI.

The sector's slow adoption is understandable. The response to it — funding only the easy-to-adopt, automation-first tools — is not. When the bar for fundable AI in democratic tech becomes "things you're already doing, but faster," we are collectively setting ourselves up to lose the most important technological moment of our generation.

### The Progressive Sector's Hidden Advantage

Here's what makes this especially frustrating: the progressive sector actually has a natural advantage in the third wave of AI — if it would recognize it.

Progressive and nonprofit organizations operate on *alternative logics* to commercial ones. They understand that change is non-linear, that it's not purely money-bound, that the people closest to problems hold the deepest insight into solutions. These are not weaknesses in the age of AI. They are precisely the logics that enable genuine value creation through human-machine collaboration.

The question is whether we'll use AI to entrench the transactional model that's already failing us, or whether we'll build tools that actually leverage our sector's greatest strengths: deep community relationships, domain expertise in organizing and movement-building, and an understanding that people are agents of democracy — not consumers of it.

## Part 3: Elevation > Automation

At Tectonica — where I've spent the last 14 years leading a company that has worked with over 600 progressive organizations across 40 countries — we've been wrestling with these questions directly. And we've arrived at a principle that we believe should guide how AI is built for the movements fighting for justice:

### Elevation > Automation

**We aim to elevate, not just to automate.**

This is not a tagline. It's a design philosophy. It represents a fundamental choice about *what AI is for* in the context of democratic practice and social change.

The logic of automation says: take what humans do, have machines do it instead, save money. The logic of elevation says: take what humans know — their expertise, their strategic wisdom, their community insight, their creative vision — and amplify it through AI so that more people can create at higher levels, more autonomously, more aligned with collective strategy. Protect and project the voices of those who are currently marginalized.

The difference matters enormously. Automation replaces the chef with a recipe generator. Elevation gives every cook in the kitchen the instincts and knowledge of the best chef on the team — while keeping the chef in the room, directing, curating, and evolving the craft.

We've been building something based on this principle. I won't go into the full details here — we're still in early stages, running pilots and learning — but the work is grounded in a set of values that I want to put on the record, because I believe they should inform how anyone in our sector approaches generative AI:

**Our Gen AI Values:**

*Counter to the assumptions of Generative AI in operation today, we believe the promise of Gen AI is fulfilled when used to:*

**Make Democracy better, not just politics cheaper.** We see the power of gen AI to enable creation, bring people together, and empower those fighting injustice — not just increase efficiency. To promote participation. To build people power, rather than a politics of transaction.

**Augment through Specialists.** Derive power from the wisdom and talents of domain specialists — don't replace them for AI slop. In the age of AI, the designer becomes a *strategic designer*, the organizer a *strategic organizer*. Their expertise becomes more valuable, not less, because it guides what the machines produce.

**Amplify diverse voices, visions, and solutions for change.** When people talk about Gen AI in politics, they often talk about deep fakes — the ability to show a reality that doesn't exist. We believe Gen AI also has the power to do the opposite: to empower those who might not otherwise have access to professional-level creation, to show the reality of their own communities in ways not seen in public discourse. Amplify the voices of diverse communities rather than dull them with the hegemonic influence of massive LLM datasets.

**Based in Human Intention and Leadership.** Gen AI is only as valuable as the intention, direction, and strategy we use to collaborate with it. Human leadership — rooted in lived experience and community values — cannot be replaced by predictive patterning and mimicry.

Additionally, we pledge to be aware of and make all efforts to mitigate the potential harms — individual and systemic — in the use of generative AI tools, and to be transparent in our use.

### Why This Matters Now

The progressive sector wants to use AI. That's clear from every conversation I've had in the last year — those organically coming to us with interest in what we are building, hundreds of meetings, conferences on multiple continents. The problem isn't *whether* but *how*. Organizations are overwhelmed, under-resourced, and rightly cautious about tools that feel disconnected from their values.

The gap isn't going to be filled by generic efficiency tools slotted into existing workflows. It's going to be filled by AI that is built *from within* the movements — embedded with the strategic expertise that comes from actually doing the work, designed to elevate the people doing the organizing rather than replace them.

This is what we're building toward. It's early. There's much still to figure out. But the principle is clear and non-negotiable: **Elevation > Automation.**

The third wave of technological transformation is here. The question is whether we'll use it to make more junk politics, or to make democracy itself work better. Whether we'll invest in tools that make campaigns cheaper, or ones that make communities stronger. Whether we'll automate the same shallow tactics that have been eroding trust for decades, or build something genuinely new — something that puts human expertise, human connection, and human agency back at the center.

We know which side we're on. And we think that's where the real returns are — for movements, for democracy, and yes, for the civic tech sector too.

---

*Ned Howey is the CEO and co-founder of Tectonica Digital Campaign Solutions, a Barcelona-based B Corp that has served 600+ progressive organizations across 40+ countries. He is the author of "T* [*he Age of Junk Politics: Fixing Our Democracies and Winning Elections*](https://www.tectonica.co/age_of_junk_politics_book) *" and the creator of the* [*Tectonica Organizing Network (TON)*](https://www.tectonica.co/ton_hub)*.*
